query: TONE trial results on erythropoietin for optic neuritis

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Neuroprotective Agent Trials (Citicoline and Erythropoietin)
retrieved_docs.content: Design: Small-scale pilot studies. Focus: Evaluate whether agents such as citicoline and erythropoietin may promote optic nerve recovery. Preliminary data: Indicate possible benefit in visual field preservation and axonal support. Status: Awaiting larger randomized controlled trials. [92]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Neuritis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: The collaborative, multicenter ONTT attempted to clarify the role of corticosteroids in acute optic neuritis treatment. [62] More than 450 patients were enrolled at 15 sites between 1988 and 1991. The average age of enrolled subjects was 32. Most participants were Caucasian (85%) and female (77%). Subjects were randomized to receive oral prednisone 1 mg/kg daily for 2 weeks or intravenous methylprednisolone 250 mg every 6 hours for 3 days, followed by either oral prednisone or an oral placebo for the remainder of the 2 weeks.

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Loss of Vision -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Ongoing Research
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of neuroprotective agents in optic neuritis: This ongoing study is assessing the efficacy of various neuroprotective agents in reducing long-term visual impairment in patients with optic neuritis. [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Loss of Vision -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Reported Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Optic neuritis: The Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial demonstrated that corticosteroids can accelerate visual recovery in patients with optic neuritis and reduce the risk of developing multiple sclerosis during the following 2 years. [66]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Neuritis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Most subjects in each cohort recovered visual function in the first 1 to 3 months. Visual improvement occurred more rapidly in subjects treated with intravenous methylprednisolone, but no difference was observed in the cohorts receiving oral corticosteroids and placebo. Visual acuity did not fully return in many subjects, with initial visual acuity of 20/200 or worse. Visual outcomes at 6 months were similar in all cohorts, but optic neuritis recurred twice as often in subjects who received prednisone.

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Ischemic Optic Neuropathy Decompression Trial
retrieved_docs.content: Design: Randomized controlled trial. Objective: To assess the efficacy of optic nerve sheath decompression surgery in NAION. Findings: The trial concluded that surgical decompression provided no visual benefit and may be harmful. Nearly 32% of patients in the surgical group lost ≥3 lines of vision, compared to 23% in the observation group. Impact: This pivotal study discouraged surgical interventions for NAION and emphasized the importance of conservative management and control of vascular risk factors. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Epoetin Alfa -- Indications -- Experimental Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Friedreich ataxia and frataxin levels: Erythropoietin increases frataxin levels in peripheral blood mononuclear cells, a key factor in Friedreich ataxia, characterized by low frataxin levels. In a 2016 randomized controlled trial of patients with Friedreich ataxia, epoetin alfa did not impact the primary endpoint of peak oxygen uptake or affect frataxin levels. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- RPh201 (Human Placental Extract) Trial
retrieved_docs.content: Phase: Phase 2a clinical trial. Objective: To assess the safety and efficacy of RPh201, a novel bioactive compound, in improving visual function in patients with NAION. Results: Preliminary data indicated a mild improvement in visual acuity in some patients. Current status: Further larger-scale studies are needed to validate results. [90]

retrieved_docs.title: Central Pontine Myelinolysis -- Treatment / Management -- Experimental Strategies
retrieved_docs.content: There have been no randomized control studies conducted to confirm the efficacy of these treatment modalities. However, they have been used in a small number of cases and have been shown to have positive outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Neuritis -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: At 1 year, no differences were found in visual function (ie, acuity, visual field, color discrimination, and contrast sensitivity) between subjects who received placebo and corticosteroids [visual acuity of 20/40 or better: placebo (95%), intravenous methylprednisolone (94%), and oral prednisone (91%)]. [63] Visual recovery, visual field results, and neurologic consequences of optic neuritis have been reported at 3 years and 15 years. [64] [65] [66] [67] [68] [69] [70] [71] [72] [73] [74] [75] [76] [77] Diagnostic and treatment guidelines for optic neuritis have evolved to help physicians differentiate typical cases from the atypical variants associated with more complex neurologic disorders. [78] [79] [80] [81]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Treatment / Management -- Investigational Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Several treatments have been evaluated in clinical trials, although none have definitively proven beneficial. Ischemic optic neuropathy decompression trial: This trial evaluated surgical optic nerve sheath decompression and found no benefit, along with potential harm from the procedure. [83] Intravitreal injections: Anti-VEGF agents, including bevacizumab or ranibizumab, are used off-label in some cases to reduce disc edema. However, supporting data are limited, and this approach is not considered standard of care. Intravitreal triamcinolone has been trialed to reduce inflammation and swelling, though evidence of benefit remains limited. Neurotrophic factors and stem cell therapy: Neurotrophic factors and stem cell therapy remain in preclinical and early-phase research stages. Currently, there are no therapies with proven efficacy. [84]

retrieved_docs.title: Opsoclonus -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Ongoing clinical trials and research initiatives include the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Recent studies have explored various pharmacological and nonpharmacological interventions, including corticosteroids, anti-vascular endothelial growth factor (anti-VEGF) agents, neuroprotective drugs, and hyperbaric oxygen therapy; however, none have demonstrated conclusive benefits in large-scale randomized trials. Clinical trials investigating neuroprotective strategies and optic nerve regeneration remain an area of ongoing research, offering hope for future interventions. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy Treatment Trial Using Brimonidine
retrieved_docs.content: Design: Multicenter randomized controlled trial. Objective: To test whether brimonidine, a neuroprotective alpha-2 agonist, improved outcomes. Outcome: No statistically significant improvement in visual acuity or visual field compared to placebo. Conclusion: The study did not support the use of brimonidine as an effective treatment for acute NAION. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonarteritic Anterior Ischemic Optic Neuropathy -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Obstructive Sleep Apnea and Continuous Positive Airway Pressure Intervention Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Context: OSA is a known risk factor for NAION. Design: Observational and interventional studies assessing the role of CPAP therapy in preventing second eye involvement. Findings: Some evidence suggests that patients using CPAP are less likely to develop NAION in the fellow eye. Implication: Sleep studies and OSA management may become part of comprehensive NAION care. [93]

retrieved_docs.title: Transient Loss of Vision -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Reported Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Transient ischemic attack: The EXPRESS study's results (Early use of EXisting PREventive Strategies for Stroke) demonstrated that rapid intervention in patients with TIA (including those presenting with transient vision loss) can substantially reduce the risk of subsequent stroke. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1530
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin, methylprednisolon acetát, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis. These therapies, which include the peptide hormone ACTH (corticotropin) and two corticosteroid formulations, are employed to modulate the immune response and reduce inflammatory activity that underlies the demyelinating process of multiple sclerosis. Their potential use reflects a therapeutic approach aimed at controlling disease activity and improving neurological outcomes in patients with this chronic autoimmune disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin, methylprednisolon acetate, and methylprednisolone aceponate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, indicating that several corticosteroid‑related medications are considered potential therapeutic options for managing this demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1823
retrieved_docs.content: Human corticotropin and several corticosteroid formulations—including methylprednisolon acetate, methylprednisolone aceponate, and methylprednisolone sodium phosphate—are identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis. These drugs act by modulating immune activity and reducing inflammatory processes that contribute to demyelination and neurological deficits in multiple sclerosis. Their use, particularly during acute relapses, may help alleviate symptoms, limit lesion formation, and improve short‑term neurological function, highlighting the role of corticosteroid‑based therapies in the management of this chronic autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2358
retrieved_docs.content: Blindness and vision defects, impulse control disorders, and the corticosteroid methylprednisolon are recognized as relevant qualifiers when assessing cerebrospinal fluid (mozkomíšní mok). Findings in cerebrospinal fluid are also pertinent to the evaluation of multiple sclerosis and to the assessment of vitamin B 12 status. Methylprednisolon may be employed as a therapeutic agent in the treatment of multiple sclerosis, and vitamin B 12 plays an important role in the management and differential diagnosis of multiple sclerosis‑related neurological dysfunction. Integrating these qualifiers and treatments enhances diagnostic accuracy and informs comprehensive patient care for conditions involving visual impairment, impulse control disturbances, and demyelinating disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1944

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1490
retrieved_docs.content: Methylprednisolone, a corticosteroid medication, may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, offering anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that can help reduce disease activity. In addition, vitamin B12 is also considered a potential therapeutic agent for multiple sclerosis, as supplementation may support neurological function and address deficiencies that can exacerbate symptoms. Both methylprednisolone and vitamin B12 represent possible treatment options in the management of multiple sclerosis, highlighting the importance of a multifaceted therapeutic approach.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1298
retrieved_docs.content: Dantrolen and vitamin B12 are both identified as potential therapeutic agents for multiple sclerosis, suggesting that each may be used to help manage this demyelinating neurological disorder. Their inclusion highlights the exploration of diverse treatment options in the care of patients with multiple sclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Procedural interventions related to this disease spectrum encompass various insertion acts: tricuspid valve replacement performed with cardiopulmonary bypass for valvular failure, insertion of sphenoidal electrodes by qualified health‑care professionals for electroencephalographic (EEG) recording, and acupuncture anesthesia as an alternative therapeutic modality. Overall, secondary diabetes mellitus, whether complicated or uncomplicated, is a central driver of retinal and renal pathology, systemic organ failure, and necessitates both ocular destructive therapies and diverse invasive procedures within comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 159
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical act of insertion comprises diverse procedural techniques such as tricuspid valve replacement with cardiopulmonary bypass, insertion of sphenoidal electrodes for electroencephalographic recording, acupuncture anesthesia, and radiation‑based destruction of localized retinal or choroidal lesions (including macular edema and tumors). In ocular disease, a specific destructive procedure on the posterior segment—radiation implantation to eradicate retinal or choroidal lesions—is a child of the general retinal lesion destruction technique and exemplifies the therapeutic use of insertion. Chest imaging performed for metastatic carcinoma of the thyroid often incorporates a radioisotope study of the endocrine system; this imaging directly targets endocrine structures, including the pancreatic hormone axis, and may be accompanied by interventions such as castration. Within the endocrine system, secondary diabetes mellitus (a subtype of diabetes mellitus without complication) is a metabolic disorder that precipitates microvascular complications: mild, moderate, and broader categories of non‑proliferative retinopathy and general retinopathy, all localized to the retina, as well as nephropathy and a specific form of diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene. The retinal complications manifest as detachments, defects, vascular occlusion, and are described in Arabic as داء ريفسام (retinopathy). Glycogenolytic hormones constitute a broader class that includes pancreatic hormones, which participate in glycogenolysis and are implicated in the glucose dysregulation of secondary diabetes mellitus. Recognizing the interconnections among insertion‑based interventions, endocrine imaging, pancreatic hormone activity, and the ocular and renal sequelae of secondary diabetes mellitus informs comprehensive diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage into nerve trunk, inflammatory and toxic neuropathy, and hereditary sensory neuropathy (اعتلال الأعصاب الحسي الوراثي) are all conditions in which the nerve serves as the primary site of pathological findings. This highlights that the nerve is a common locus for diverse neuropathic processes, ranging from acute hemorrhagic injury to inflammatory, toxic, and genetic sensory neuropathies. Recognizing the nerve as the affected structure helps clinicians focus diagnostic evaluation and tailor management strategies for patients presenting with these nerve‑related disorders.
